title: Services, Units, Boot State, and Logs · Mastery Check
summary: Diagnose a service that is enabled but unavailable by separating desired boot state, current process state, dependencies, configuration, and logs. Every item uses a fresh case and misconception-specific distractors. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for a service lifecycle and journal evidence packet. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Relate service managers, units, dependencies, targets, process supervision, and application readiness.
Use status, is-active, is-enabled, show, list-dependencies, and failed-unit evidence before changing service state.
Query journal fields, units, boots, priorities, time ranges, cursors, and follow mode without losing causal context.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# linux-basics cumulative operations evidence notebook
# Commands run only in the deterministic stateful terminal simulator.
# Record hypothesis, command, expected signal, observation, conclusion, and recovery.

steps.title: Service Unit Model · changed case
steps.instruction:
quiz case — lab-worker.service is enabled and active, but readiness failed after configuration revision 4. Query effective unit settings and journal events from this boot before any restart or rollback.

Which action demonstrates Service Unit Model without dropping earlier contracts?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for relate service managers, units, dependencies, targets, process supervision, and application readiness.

content.text: lab-worker.service is enabled and active, but readiness failed after configuration revision 4. Query effective unit settings and journal events from this boot before any restart or rollback.

content.title: Commit before feedback
content.text: Choose from the stated data before opening the worked model. Record which Service Unit Model condition decided your prediction.

content.label: Authority to consult after committing
content.value: GNU Bash Reference Manual — Controls Bash parsing, expansion, quoting, redirection, pipelines, startup, job control, scripting, and error behavior.

hints: Name the Service Unit Model precondition or invariant that the quiz case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: lab-worker.service is enabled and active, but readiness failed after configuration revision 4. Query effective unit settings and journal events from this boot before any restart or rollback.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: An enabled service is necessarily running and healthy now.

options.text: For service unit model · changed case, apply Service Unit Model without its stated preconditions, omit the a service lifecycle and journal evidence packet trace and counterexample, and treat one plausible output as complete proof. This does not satisfy Service Unit Model for a service lifecycle and journal evidence packet.

steps.title: Diagnose the hidden failure

quiz case — lab-worker.service is enabled and active, but readiness failed after configuration revision 5. Query effective unit settings and journal events from this boot before any restart or rollback. Competency probe: Define restart limits, readiness checks, dependency order, failure escalation, and rollback evidence for a service change.

The visible result looks correct, but its invariant or value contract is unverified. What must happen next?

stimulus.title: Service Recovery failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: Automatic infinite restart is a complete availability strategy.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Define restart limits, readiness checks, dependency order, failure escalation, and rollback evidence for a service change.

options.text: For diagnose the hidden failure, apply Service Recovery without its stated preconditions, omit the a service lifecycle and journal evidence packet trace and counterexample, and treat one plausible output as complete proof. This does not satisfy Service Recovery for a service lifecycle and journal evidence packet.
Run the Service Recovery boundary case for a service lifecycle and journal evidence packet, inspect the actual mechanism, then accept or repair the claim.
Accept the Service Recovery result because visible a service lifecycle and journal evidence packet output is the highest authority. This does not resolve diagnose the hidden failure.

steps.title: Defend one difficult decision
steps.instruction: Write the quiz evidence note for Service Recovery: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — lab-worker.service is enabled and active, but readiness failed after configuration revision 6. Query effective unit settings and journal events from this boot before any restart or rollback. Competency probe: Define restart limits, readiness checks, dependency order, failure escalation, and rollback evidence for a service change.
steps.why: Retrieving Service Recovery through the concrete a service lifecycle and journal evidence packet case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Define restart limits, readiness checks, dependency order, failure escalation, and rollback evidence for a service change.

hints: Name the Service Recovery precondition or invariant that the quiz case must preserve.
